quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Limit"?

options.text: The value a function approaches as the input approaches a particular value: lim(x→c) f(x) = L; the function need not equal L at x = c
f'(x) > 0: f is increasing (quantity growing); f'(x) < 0: f is decreasing (quantity declining)
A function can have more than one point satisfying MVT; each is where the tangent is parallel to the secant
f⁻¹ reverses f: if f(a) = b, then f⁻¹(b) = a; domain of f⁻¹ = range of f, and vice versa

quiz_questions.explanation: "Limit" is defined as: The value a function approaches as the input approaches a particular value: lim(x→c) f(x) = L; the function need not equal L at x = c. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "One-sided limits"?

options.text: Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
EVT guarantees extrema exist but does not tell us where; we use critical point analysis and endpoint evaluation to locate them
The set of input values that make physical sense in the context; endpoints may produce absolute extrema

quiz_questions.explanation: "One-sided limits" is defined as: Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Continuity"?

options.text: A point where concavity changes; f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND the sign of f''(x) changes
State the integral being evaluated, show the antiderivative, substitute limits, and simplify; credit for setup even if computation errors occur
A function f is continuous at c if f(c) is defined, lim(x→c) f(x) exists, and lim(x→c) f(x) = f(c)
∫csc²x dx = -cot x + C; ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C; ∫csc x cot x dx = -csc x + C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Continuity" is defined as: A function f is continuous at c if f(c) is defined, lim(x→c) f(x) exists, and lim(x→c) f(x) = f(c). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Intermediate Value Theorem"?

options.text: A relation where y is defined implicitly by an equation F(x,y) = 0 rather than explicitly as y = f(x)
x-intercepts, y-intercept, local maxima and minima, inflection points, and behavior at asymptotes and endpoints
If f is continuous on [a,b] and k is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a,b) where f(c) = k
∫csc²x dx = -cot x + C; ∫sec x tan x dx = sec x + C; ∫csc x cot x dx = -csc x + C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Intermediate Value Theorem" is defined as: If f is continuous on [a,b] and k is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a,b) where f(c) = k. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Removable discontinuity"?

options.text: Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
A point where the limit exists but either f(c) is undefined or f(c) ≠ lim(x→c) f(x); can be 'fixed' by redefining f(c)
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two
The interval must include both endpoints; on open intervals (a,b), extrema may be approached but not attained

quiz_questions.explanation: "Removable discontinuity" is defined as: A point where the limit exists but either f(c) is undefined or f(c) ≠ lim(x→c) f(x); can be 'fixed' by redefining f(c). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The value a function approaches as the input approaches a particular value: lim(x→c) f(x) = L; the function need not equal L at x = c"?

options.text: Combining with u-substitution
Limit
Left/Right/Midpoint sum
Second derivative confirmation

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Limit".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal"?

options.text: One-sided limits
Linearity
F increasing/decreasing
Epsilon (ε)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "One-sided limits".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A function f is continuous at c if f(c) is defined, lim(x→c) f(x) exists, and lim(x→c) f(x) = f(c)"?

options.text: Continuity
Second derivative confirmation
Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
Infinite limit

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Continuity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If f is continuous on [a,b] and k is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists at least one c in (a,b) where f(c) = k"?

options.text: Linearity
When the test fails
Multiple chain rule
Intermediate Value Theorem

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Intermediate Value Theorem".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A point where the limit exists but either f(c) is undefined or f(c) ≠ lim(x→c) f(x); can be 'fixed' by redefining f(c)"?

options.text: Combined technique
Removable discontinuity
Related rates method
Shadow problems

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Removable discontinuity".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Limits and Continuity is accurate?

options.text: For logarithms: d/dx[ln x] = 1/x and d/dx[logₐ x] = 1/(x ln a).
When k > 0: exponential growth (population, investment); when k < 0: exponential decay (radioactive decay, drug clearance).
The fundamental pair: d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = -sin x (derived from the limit definition using lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1).
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Limits and Continuity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Limits and Continuity?

options.text: Choose u and dv from the integrand using the LIATE priority: Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential (choose u from earlier in the list).
A limit describes the value a function approaches as the input approaches a specific value: lim(x→c) f(x) = L means f(x) gets arbitrarily close to L as x approaches c.
This yields the derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions: d/dx[arcsin x] = 1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arccos x] = -1/√(1-x²), d/dx[arctan x] = 1/(1+x²).
Formal Definition of Limits (Epsilon-Delta) provides the rigorous mathematical foundation for limits.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Limits and Continuity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Limits and Continuity?

options.text: A function is continuous at x = c if: (1) f(c) exists, (2) lim(x→c) f(x) exists, and (3) lim(x→c) f(x) = f(c).
The solution is P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) where A = (K-P₀)/P₀.

Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).
A slope field (direction field) displays short line segments at grid points (x,y), each with slope equal to f(x,y) from dy/dx = f(x,y).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Limits and Continuity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Limits and Continuity?

options.text: The Intermediate Value Theorem states that a continuous function on [a,b] takes every value between f(a) and f(b).
The chain rule 'peels off layers' of composition: differentiate the outer function (leaving inner unchanged), then multiply by the derivative of the inner.
Visualizing MVT helps interpret derivative graphs and connect graphical and analytical representations.
Differential Equation Basics introduces equations involving derivatives.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Limits and Continuity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Limits and Continuity?

options.text: If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.
As n→∞, Riemann sums converge to the definite integral: ∫ₐᵇf(x)dx = lim(n→∞) Σf(xᵢ*)Δx.
Antiderivatives: Inverse Trigonometric Forms produces inverse trig functions from specific integrand patterns.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Limits and Continuity as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Limits and Continuity?

options.text: IVT guarantees a root exists: if f(a)  0 for continuous f, there must be a zero between a and b—used in numerical root-finding algorithms
Error propagation bounds: if a measuring instrument's response function has bounded derivative, MVT limits the maximum output error from a given input error
AC electrical circuits: voltage V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt) has derivative V'(t) = V₀ω cos(ωt), the rate of voltage change used in capacitor/inductor analysis
Computer-aided design (CAD): finding normal vectors to complex curved surfaces defined implicitly requires combined differentiation techniques

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Limits and Continuity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Limits and Continuity?

options.text: Computer algebra systems: software like Wolfram Alpha uses algorithmic decision trees similar to this strategy to compute symbolic integrals automatically
GPS navigation continuously tracking position: mathematically modeled as a continuous function of time, allowing interpolation between data points
AC electrical circuits: voltage V(t) = V₀ sin(ωt) has derivative V'(t) = V₀ω cos(ωt), the rate of voltage change used in capacitor/inductor analysis
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Limits and Continuity.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Limits and Continuity applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Temperature modeling: since temperature changes continuously, the IVT guarantees that between any two measured temperatures, every intermediate value was achieved
Quality control: setting an acceptable error margin (ε) for a product property and determining the input precision (δ) needed in the manufacturing process
Climate data: identifying periods of warming (increasing temperature trend) vs. cooling using derivative analysis of temperature time series
Market saturation: adoption of new technology (smartphones, streaming services) follows an S-curve as the product approaches market saturation

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Limits and Continuity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Limits and Continuity?

options.text: Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0
Control systems engineering: inverse tangent functions model phase shifts in frequency response, with derivatives indicating sensitivity to frequency changes
IVT guarantees a root exists: if f(a)  0 for continuous f, there must be a zero between a and b—used in numerical root-finding algorithms
Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Limits and Continuity.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Limits and Continuity in practice?

options.text: Error analysis in numerical methods: understanding limits helps predict how rounding errors accumulate as step sizes approach zero in computer calculations
Climate data: identifying periods of warming (increasing temperature trend) vs. cooling using derivative analysis of temperature time series
Market saturation: adoption of new technology (smartphones, streaming services) follows an S-curve as the product approaches market saturation
GPS navigation continuously tracking position: mathematically modeled as a continuous function of time, allowing interpolation between data points

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Limits and Continuity.
